displayTitle: 2026-06-15 - Social Services and Community Committee - 2026/27 Estimates for Vote Arts, Culture and Heritage
topic: 2026/27 Estimates for Vote Arts, Culture and Heritage
description: Monday, 15 June 2026 | Social Services and Community Committee 2026/27 Estimates for Vote Arts, Culture and Heritage Hearing of Evidence: Minister for Arts, Culture and Heritage and Minister for Media and Communications Hon Paul Goldsmith 2026/27 Estimates for Vote Pacific Peoples Minister for Pacific Peoples Hon Paul Goldsmith
descriptionSummary: 2026/27 Estimates for Vote Arts, Culture and Heritage. Minister for Arts, Culture and Heritage and Minister for Media and Communications. Hon Paul Goldsmith

chunks.text: All right. Good afternoon, minister and officials. Welcome to the Social Services and Community Select Committee. We have you for an hour this afternoon on this appropriation in your role as Minister for Arts, Culture and Heritage, and obviously the department. So look, welcome. If you want to introduce yourselves and then kick off with introductory comments, and then we'll move to questions.

Good. Thank you very much, Mr. Chair. I'm covering the media as well, aren't I? Or... Yes. Arts, Culture and Heritage and Media. Is that right? I think it's, yes, exactly. Yes. Yep. Okay. Very good. Well, thank you very much for the invitation. I've got my chief executive here, Mac, and the deputy secretary and various others, if there are any particular questions that you have that we want to get into. I thought I'd start off with just recalling some of the highlights of the wider sector.

And we think of, I want to acknowledge Kate Hawley, for her excellent Academy Award-winning work with costume design for Phantom. Mm. I think we need to acknowledge that success. I think we should also acknowledge Apirana Mahu Pae, with the great success of his film, Tina, going around the world. The Ockham Book Award winner, Nathanua Persall-Kecsey should be greatly successful. And then just in the broader context of if you think of the vibrancy of the sector, the Aotearoa Art Fair, which I attended, was the biggest yet, with 65 galleries from 25 countries and more than 200 works on

display. So a lot going on in the arts, creative, and cultural sector. I could name many other things. But I think if we were to draw a conclusion, I would say that many New Zealanders continue to do both well here in New Zealand, but also on the global stage.

The Budget 2026 continues our very, very significant investment in the arts, culture, and heritage space, $460 million in '26, '27, $1.7 billion over four years. I suppose one of the more significant things in the- Budget was having secured 185 million to ensure the domestic screen production rebate can continue, and that's something that has been budgeted for at a modest level for a number of years, and every year there's been a scramble and a top-up.

And so we've put that into a more appropriate setting, because it's a key growth area for New Zealand, supporting jobs and international connections. We've made some savings over the four-year period, a 2% ask for saving across the board, as we have right across our government.

And it would be nice to come into this role with a budget surplus and lots of money to spend. That, unfortunately, has never been our experience on this side of the House. We usually come in with a big deficit, which we're trying to climb our way out of, and leave the surplus for others to enjoy.

So we hope and pray that this time we'll be blessed with being in power long enough to enjoy the surpluses when they come, and to be able to invest some further funds. But in the meantime, we have to be careful with our money, and that's what we're doing. What else can I say?

Just in terms of the broader creative setting, you will recall last year Amplify was the strategy document that we worked towards with three key pillars. Investing for maximum impact, making sure that the money that we do put in, that 400-odd million, is leveraging also private sector and philanthropic investment. Secondly, nurturing talent, building that pipeline of talent, and connecting well with the education sector on that.

And then finally, reducing barriers to growth, thinking about that regulatory context, and there's much we can talk about there if people are interested. So I'll leave it at that and offer myself up to some questioning. Thank you, Minister. First question, Rachel. Thank you, Chair. Thank you, Minister. So I'd like to ask some questions around the cuts in this year's budget, and just to indicate to the Chair, I'd like some sustained- Sustained... questioning.

We will. As many as we can. Yeah. So you mentioned at the beginning that there's continued investment in the arts, but there's significant cuts this time in the budget. So my first question is around cuts to Creative New Zealand. We've seen cuts to their budget, and as a result, 23 jobs proposed to be cut from Creative New Zealand.

So my first question is, how do you expect Creative New Zealand to be able to Amplify, deliver on your objectives, and support the growth of the creative industry when you're cutting 23 jobs from that organization? Well, thank you for the question. I think you got the word "cut" in five times.

And I think what people will need to be conscious of, the fact is that what we're talking about is 2%, and savings that has been required right across government. And I just remind people who are tuning in on their TV screens and their crystal sets across the country listening in to this debate, that notwithstanding all the savings that we've made, this budget is still forecasting an $11.4 billion deficit. So as a country, we're spending more than we're earning.

And we inherited very substantial deficits when we came into power in 2023. And we're trying to get a path back to surplus, which is what we left the country in 2017 when we last were in power. And why does that matter? Because if you're running up big deficits every year, you're effectively asking the next generation to pay for the things that you're enjoying today.

So we're asking our kids and our grandkids to pay for stuff that we're doing today, and we don't think that's appropriate. That's why we've got a plan to get back to surplus. And that's not easy. It is much easier, as Ms. Boyack's colleagues will know when they were in government, it's much easier just to throw money out there, run up a deficit, borrow more money, and get lots of applause and thanks from everybody. It's much harder to be more disciplined in our spending.

So that's what we're doing. And in relation to Creative New Zealand, of course, what we're doing, and I've appointed an excellent chair there, and the board has appointed a new CEO. And my simple focus there is that over the last couple of decades, the amount that Creative New Zealand has spent on their administration, as opposed to money that goes out the door to artists, has grown and grown and grown. And so what we've asked the new leadership team is there to reduce the amount that they're spending on administration

so that more money can go out the door. And so my expectation is that we'll get an extra $4 million out the door to artists from the rearrangements that have been made at Creative New Zealand. I think that is the right thing to do. I want to dig into this actually, because I met with a group of artists in Palmerston North last week about the changes at a regional level, and their concern is they were given only one month to submit an ROI on administering the new system that's been set up.

And they have a lot of unanswered questions, one of which is, there will be an administrative cost to actually giving out that money through the regions. How is that going to be paid for? Or is the money going into that new regional approach only going to be the money that gets to artists, and those organizations expected to actually deliver the administrative burden themselves?

Well, I imagine that'll be details of the ROI that Creative New Zealand is working their way through, and there'll be a range of- There'll be a range of options put forward. But yes, our expectation is that putting the funding closer to the community, and using organizations that are currently already set up to distribute funds in many respects, it varies in different regions, will be a more efficient way of doing it, rather than having a very large apparatus in Wellington taking up a very substantial slice of the money available.

Mm-hmm. And we want to see more of it out the door to the artists. So why would those regions that don't have organizations already established, like Palmerston North, Manawatu, only given one month for an ROI, where they're actually having to set up a brand new trust in order to deliver this model? Well, that's a question that can be asked to Creative New Zealand's board. I don't have the details of how long they particularly allow the process to develop and we can come back to you with some reasons on that. That's not the degree of detail that I'd get into.

Okay. Supplementary. Yeah, look, I've heard also over many, many years from people who are looking to apply for funding for Creative New Zealand, it's a very, very bureaucratic process, and can be really onerous. What I'm really interested in is what the balance of funding has been. You said obviously too much money went to the bureaucracy, if you like, and too little went out the door in terms of grants.

But if we can track what it looks like over time in terms of the growth of the bureaucracy, then starting turning it down again- Yes... versus the trajectory for actually grants getting out on the ground. Well, that's very much what the challenge that I've given to the board, and they're very upfront and they're doing a great job.

And so they're working their way through a process at the moment. But the goal, like I say, is to get at least an extra $4 million out the door to artists, in grants of the wide variety, whether it's music, whether it's poetry, whether it's writing or drama or you name it.

All those things are getting that money out the door in terms of grants, rather than being spent on detailed administration here in Wellington. Still leaving substantial overheads for administration. But I just think over time it had grown and it needed to be pruned back. And you'd expect to be able to report and track that? Yes. Good. A supplementary on this, Minister, and it's just a discussion about cuts, sort of got me thinking about an orchard. In an orchard, you sometimes need to prune it so that you can have actually better growth going forward.

So you've kind of touched on that with more than $4 million. But I'm just wondering, what is the thinking of yourself and the ministry about pruning the orchard, so to speak, so that you can have better, stronger growth in the creative sector? It's not necessarily a metaphor that I would use. My simple view, which is one that's shared by the Creative New Zealand board, is that there's two ways you can spend your money and how it's developed is, one is that there is the administrative overhead of running Creative New

Zealand and having an office in Wellington and employing a number of people. And then the second element is grants that are put out to the artistic community. And my concern is that over time, that proportion on administration had grown out of whack with the amount that was going out the door in terms of grants. Now, there had been a big increase, of course, during COVID when there'd been extra money sloshing around in the system, but it hadn't come back down fast enough.

And so our simple objective is to change that ratio so that less is being spent at administration in Wellington, more is going out the door to the artists- Good... directly. And that's what we're setting out to achieve, and I think that's the right thing to do. Thank you. Ka rangi. Your question. Thank you. Can you confirm that the 27.5 million that's been cut over- Over four years.

So the 27.5 million is that cuts over the four years. And then, thank you. And then over the three years, the Ministry of Culture and Heritage is expected to find a total savings of 150 million. So that's, if we take off that 27.5 million, we've got 122 million, and that's about an average of 57.8 million per year.

That's including these cuts of 2% in the first year to the public services, 5% in the second, and 5% in the third year. So could you just confirm that as well as that 27.5 million, there is another 122 million-ish that needs to be cut? Well, that is the future predictions as announced in terms of the overall public sector.

We're trying to, A, reduce the overall headcount back to somewhere close to, again just for people tuning in, if we looked at the core public service, 47,000 people employed in 2017. We come back six years later and it's 63,000. It's grown by a very substantial amount.

Meantime, the population has grown, so some growth is justified. So our proposal is to get back to 55,000. Not all the way back to 47, but back to 55. And we think we can do that and still have a fine, productive, and efficient public service. So that's one element. Another element is the projections that the Minister of Finance and I, wearing another hat, Public Service Commissioner, talked about savings of 2% this year and then 5% for the next two years. The out- Flow of that is not detailed in this budget, because

it's very much a discussion still to be had. And there may be some areas where it's easier to find those savings, particularly areas where there's been significant increases over the last few years. There may be other areas where it won't be appropriate, and so we haven't made those decisions. So at the moment, you don't have any sense on how those further 122 million in cuts is going to impact artists?

Well, we haven't made the decisions to actually do that yet. What we've indicated is right across government, we want to have that savings. And how it's going to roll out across the various departments and different sort of categories of spending is yet to be decided. That's a real challenge for us. And I might add, not easy.

And like I say, much easier to have lots of extra money to throw around, but that is not something that we've found ourselves with the ability. Thank you, Minister. I can see in the summary of initiatives that it's clear that it has 2% cuts for year one of the budget, 5% cuts for year two, and 5% for year three.

And so that's what I'm asking. Can you give us any indication of the impact that that's going to have on artists? John, did you want to clarify something? That's correct. In the summary of initiatives, there is 113.5 million budget savings that has to be ascertained.

As ministers outlined, we're still having to work through that process. So it is premature to start talking through what the impact will be until we have, and worked through with the cultural system how we would divvy up- Kia ora... the savings in relation to- And I thank you. And I understand that 5% and 5%, because you also administer to sport and recreation.

That's right. Do you know what split that would be? Like, would it be- No. All those decisions have yet to be made. They're decisions for ministers to make when we look across the whole of the government sector. Everybody's been tasked. We've got to make this 5% and 5% across the board. But there are many discussions to be had as to exactly where that will fall and where.

Thank you, Minister. And so there's a lot of water to go under the bridge. I think it's- Thank you. A question, Maureen. Okay. Thank you. Can I have a go, Maureen? Minister, I'll just move on to media and communications. I note that-- and this is a decision that's been made about the Broadcasting Standards Authority. So I'm assuming there will be some savings made from the disestablishment of that.

Have you given any thought to whether that will be reprioritized within the vote for- Yes... news media or arts, culture, and heritage? No, we haven't made the final decisions on that yet. But yes, there will be modest savings associated with that. But the BSA is not a huge department. It's about a million dollars a year.

And so we'll be looking at that. We've obviously got to pass legislation to repeal the legislation that sustains the BSA, the Broadcasting Standards Authority, first. And there are a couple of nuances that need to be sort of dealt with there because it interacts with some court procedures and the electoral reform and various other things. So, that's what we're working our way through, and we'll have the legislation in the House.

Once we pass that and the BSA is disestablished, then it may well be that we've got some resources freed up. It's very nice. The story that one most often hears in government is new departments being created, which is all very good, but it shouldn't just be a ratchet always going up and more and more. Sometimes there is an opportunity to say, "Well, actually, we no longer need to do that anymore, and we can live without it." And so that's what we've done there.

A supplementary on that, Minister. What is your thinking around this new media landscape and sort of mechanisms for people to have some framework if they don't think someone has represented their position or views appropriately- Yeah, well-... in the public sphere? Yes.

What's your thinking for the best approach going forward? Well, the normal laws of defamation and things like that continue to apply, obviously. So, that is a legal framework with which anybody who's talking in the public sphere needs to be conscious of.

And then ultimately, we have a choice between trying to regulate speech across the board, and as is obvious to everybody, the way that we get our information and entertainment has changed very dramatically. It used to be just these were the broadcasters and these were the papers, and that was that.

But now you've got this whole new world of TikTok, and YouTube, and podcasts, and all sorts of things. Now we can try and regulate every iteration of that, down to every TikToker, or we can realize that actually, maybe the world's changed quite substantially. People have got a whole range or suite of options available to them. And if they don't like it, probably the best solution is to turn it off and listen to something else, and that basic market force operates. And then we recognize that, well, yeah, but there are some people

who naturally think it's quite appropriate to have a higher level of standard for journalists and people who call themselves journalists. And so our sort of working assumption is that the Media Council would be the regulator for those. And people who want to join up and say, "I'm a journalist, these are my-"

Um, hold myself to a higher standard, and I'm accountable to a process through the Media Council. And then there will be some people who won't want to do that, and then they'll have to explain to their viewers or their listeners why not. And again, people will have a choice whether or not they listen to them or take them seriously. And that's my suggestion.

All right. Thank you. Right, new question. Andy. Yeah, look, I was interested in asking about the commemoration of past military campaigns. There are two particular areas I'm interested in. One is the Ngā Tapuwae program, which is... I was listening at Featherston the other day to Chris Pugsley and co, and they basically said that we've basically backed away from what was done during the World War I commemorations. And now the app, I think, is no longer operative, and the website's a bit clunky.

And obviously, we say we will remember them, but it makes it darn sight harder to remember people when those functions are not being backed up. And if I can throw the second one in there as well, the carillon in Wellington. A huge amount of money being spent on an enormously important building. The ability to play the bells, and I've noted that it's not a very common skill, and I've noted that there doesn't seem to be any money to actually pay for anybody to actually play the carillon when it's operating. So I'd be really interested in both of those, because obviously you want

to save money but can't overdo it. In relation to the second one, I can confirm that I have played the carillon. Have you? You could have a gig then. But I can't necessarily claim to have played it well. And I have to confess, I was a little skeptical about all the money being put into earthquake strengthening until I went in and saw the bells, which are- They're quite heavy... reasonably heavy, yes. Very heavy, in fact.

And so I have a greater understanding of that now. But in terms of the details of around, we had a carillon- Carillonnist... carillonnist for a long period of time, and he's retired. And so having a replacement, and probably we don't have an apprenticeship devoted to that. And so that's a task that I'm sure Mac can give us some details.

You're on. So on the carillonnist, we're working with the National School of Music, working with Wellington City Council to look at building a pipeline of talent to help play that. We have recitals that are going to be played once a month. We're working closely with the Carillon School of Australia, Society of Australia, sorry. On the Ngā Tapuwae, we're not forgetting anyone.

It's digitally archived, which is a little different to when you just archive. So digitally archiving means that all the information is still accessible and still available. Yeah. And on the app, the app actually was decommissioned back in 2019/20. Yeah. Because it was the process around licensing and editing and coding was really expensive, and there wasn't the uptake. So those are the two issues there.

Thank you. New question. Ora nei. Tena koe, Minister. I just have a couple of questions. Really, one question, but two different entities. And the question is, what impact will the $27 million worth of shavings, I'll call it shavings, from this particular budget over the next four years, what impact, if any, will that have on Te Matatini?

Well, the 2% savings that we've asked across the board do include Te Matatini, like everybody else. Everybody's got to have a chance to find those savings. But Matatini, of course, continues to be, if we look at this period of government, of the coalition government, the single biggest area of new and additional funding was to Matatini, which is now funded at, was 19 million, now 18 million after the savings, and continues to be a very significant investment.

But because it allies so fundamentally with our core strategy around Amplify, which is growing the number of New Zealanders that engage with the arts in the broader sense, because it is extraordinarily popular, and draws in communities and people. I went to some of the regional ones a couple of months ago.

Big crowds, big engagement with the arts in that capacity. And then secondly, of course, it also has huge opportunities for projecting our culture on the global stage. And we see Te Matatini-- Well, we see the kapa haka experts traveling the world and in high demand.

So, yes, they'll need to find the 2% savings like everybody else, but they continue to be funded at a substantial level. Supplementary. Actually, no, it's not a supplementary. It's about the second entity that I would wish to ask a question on behalf of, and that is Te Papa. Te Papa has enormous responsibility. In particular, there's a repatriation program that has been running for many years.

What impacts will these cuts have on the ability of Te Papa, not Maui 2.0, which I do understand received $10 million funding in this year's budget. We're talking specifically about the work that Te Papa is responsible for and doing, as you say, what this government is committed to, which is the promotion and the amplification of our culture to international audiences.

Yep. Well, Te Papa likewise needs to find that 2% saving. See, the thing is, we could've I had a choice. I could say, "Well, the Minister of Finance has asked for a 2% saving across the sector." I could've said, "Well, let's exempt XYZ," and then the rest end up having to find 5% savings.

Or if I exempt XYZ and A, B, and C, then the rest have to do 10%. And so I figured 2% is manageable across the board, and so everybody's got to find their way. And so my very strong-- I have an excellent new chair in Te Papa, an excellent board, and they are very focused on ensuring that the resources that Te Papa has is focused on the delivery of good content, new exhibitions that they're pushing out into the world.

But the focus on also raising funds, so we've enabled them, of course, to charge international visitors. And that's developed a new stream of income. They have considerable facilities available to them for exhibitions and things such as that, as well as the opportunity for greater use of philanthropy.

So they're going to have to work hard, but we believe they'll be able to do a good job. Supplementary, Louise. I think you've almost answered my question, Minister, but it was around what encouragement or incentives are there for all of these entities to become more commercial and to create income rather than rely on government funding.

Well, there'll always be a role for government funding. We'll continue to do that. But yes, a core part of our Amplify strategy is to leverage that government investment through other sources. If you think of the Heritage New Zealand area, for example, they have some considerable assets in my neck of the woods in Newmarket, a historic place right in the center of Newmarket. There are opportunities to also raise some money through wise use of that property in order to fund the preservation of those things.

In reference to heritage, of course, it's worth remembering, again, going back to that Amplify strategy and removing of regulatory controls, the most useful thing we can do in the heritage space is something that we have done as this government, which is bring some common sense back to the earthquake strengthening legislation. We can spend a poultice on stuff, but if we haven't got some common sense in that law, then we'd always be chasing our tail and heritage will be neglected and having demolition by neglect in this country.

So that's the number one thing I've done for heritage, and I'm very pleased that we've managed to achieve that. Supplementary, Rachel. Thank you. I've got a supplementary in relation to Te Papa and private funding. I've been copied into a letter from the Te Papa Foundation to you, registering their concerns about the donation cap.

Yes. And the concern that this will have a chilling effect on the ability for private philanthropy to support Te Papa. And so I'm wondering if you've got a response to that, just given you've mentioned that's an important part of supporting Te Papa. Yep. But the foundation are concerned they won't be able to do that with a change to that cap. Yes, and we've heard those concerns.

Of course, people are able to donate $100,000 under the change that we've made. It still gets a full rebate. It's over $100,000 and we've certainly heard that concern. Supplementary, Caroline. Thank you. So Minister, you talked about that you're confident that they'll still be able to be good outputs.

Do you think that the arts community who will be able to still output at the same level that they are now with the cuts? Well, obviously, the creative sector is not entirely reliant on government. I went to TAFE, and there was a man there who was a nature or whatever, and stood up and said when they got their award, "If no one's going to help you, just do it yourself." I thought that was quite a good quote.

And if you've got great music or art, people will find a way to take their creativity to the world. What we do, though, have is many opportunities to support those as best we can. But as a country, of course, we've got to live within our means, and so we've got to be careful about how we do that.

And so my goal and the focus of the ministry and the focus of the boards across the sector is to make sure that we get the best outcome with the resources that we've got, and that we're not wasting any potential. And so where it's appropriate, people get the help they can get. But in the meantime, I've got every confidence that where there's a will, there's always a way.

Thank you, Minister. Just a supplementary on those cuts, because we know that in the '25, '26 budget, Ngā Taonga Sound and Vision, we've had seven roles, and they hadn't reached that. They'd only done 75% of the storing of our culture, of our stories.

And so I think it was around seven roles that were disestablished, a kaupapa Maori group as well that had only been just brought in two years before. So looking forward to this budget, we've got more cuts- And that was sort of a project that had come to an end rather than a- A project that started with 75%, knowing that only 75% would be completed, so maybe was expecting the last 25 to be completed as well. So I guess it's the way we look at it.

But if I'm talking about that Ngā Taonga Sound and Vision, another 208,000 of cuts during this '26, '27. So, with that on top of the cuts already happening, are they going to at least the completion of... And we're talking about our old archives that are being digitalized, right? And so they're things like TV, they're things like having older people speaking, and some of that I'd say look, that's a wonderful thing, and what we've managed to achieve with the archiving, digitization of the archives is a

treasure trove for future generations. And we've managed to achieve a lot, and they'll continue to achieve a lot. It's just, on behalf of people in New Zealand, we're asking for a 2% saving. And, I think many people tuning in, some of whom might run their own businesses around the country, and they'll often find that revenue drops by 2% for a whole host of reasons.

It might be the petrol prices have gone up, or there's something going on internationally or something's that... And people have to be able to adjust and live within the means available. Like I say, I'd far rather be in this chair in a situation with a big surplus and a big increase to arts funding, and that would be a wonderful thing to offer. But that, unfortunately, is not where we found ourselves.

We find ourselves in a $12 billion hole that we're trying to climb our way out of. And we've got to get a path back to surplus. And once we've done that, once we've got some growth back in the economy, and then we've got more choices about what we spend our money on. I'll ask one last supplementary, Minister. In 2024, it was estimated that the arts and creative sector contributed $17.5 billion to the New Zealand economy.

How much of that was because the government was paying for it, and how much of it was because the creative sector was actually generating income for the government? What figure did you have there? I've seen some figures- $17.5 billion economic contribution estimated in 2024. All the focus sort of on these questions seems to be on the government propping up the arts and creative sector.

And my question is focused on how much the arts and creative sector actually generating income itself as a contribution to New Zealand's economic picture. Yes, well, there's quite a broad definition of the arts and creative sector. I think it might-- I don't know if it includes politicians because we tend to be a bit creative sometimes, but um, the, um...

No, the point you make is a good one, which is to say, not everybody who is a writer or a musician or a, across the board, is reliant on government funding. And many people find a little niche, and find some way to create a market, and get it out there. And it would be my observation of the creative sector generally is that there's an enormous amount of creativity that goes into the development of the product, whether it's a string quartet or a play or a book.

And my challenge sometimes to parts of the sector is, don't forget you need to put an equal amount of creativity into the task of persuading people to buy it or to go to it or to see it or to come along and visit it. And that's just a fact of life. You've got to do that because funding should follow, to some extent, where the people are wanting to engage with it.

And so engaging an audience is a fundamental part of any arts community. Maureen, was it last supplementary? Last, last supplementary Last, last. Well, you just reminded me about it. But, Minister, when you talk about the amount that is generated from arts, culture, and heritage, how do you actually track that?

How do you know, as a minister, how many albums are sold or- Some, somebody comes up with a figure and I'm not sure exactly who, but from time to time there is a survey done and a figure is put out there and that's one of the measures that we use for progress. But I'd be happy to come back to you with the details of exactly who's done it and when they're next likely to do it, if you'd like.

That would be helpful. Thank you. New question, Rachel. Thank you. I've got a new primary question. I think given how broad this conversation's been, could have been a sup. You've talked about living within our means. One question, not commentary, please. And, yep. We've talked about living within our means. Can you understand the frustration of local artists when they're seeing cuts to Creative New Zealand, cuts to NZSO, cuts to Te Matatini, cuts to the ballet, but $40 million going into an events fund to support Robbie Williams, Linkin Park, Post Malone,

so international artists to come and play in New Zealand, where there are cuts going into support for local New Zealand artists to grow their skills and their audiences? Well, look, yes, it is a challenge, for the broader sector. Like most of the government, we're needing to find savings, and savings are never easy.

But they are necessary in the context that we have. In terms of economic development, I mean, you might say the same thing about putting extra money into the film sector, which is what this budget has done. And ultimately, governments on behalf of New Zealanders have to make a judgment about when to invest in economic development activities designed to draw people into the country and to get a level of activity following a period of um- Frankly, recession that we'd had over a period of time.

And so, that is what the economic development investment had been about, getting some activity back into our cities, drawing tourism from across the country. And as part of that, the big event has been part of that process. And yes, it can be rightly challenged as to, is that the best way to spend the money?

But we as a government resolved that we needed some stimulus during that time. And then we can carry on having discussions about where it's best to go forward. Have you considered just-- I've got one something else. Have you considered having a visa in place so that when those international artists come to New Zealand, there is a requirement for New Zealand artists to actually be on the ticket so that they get exposure and access to their licensing revenue through the likes of that club?

Well, yes, we have considered those sorts of settings. It is quite often the case that a New Zealand artist is involved in the pre-production or not pre-production, but the curtain raiser, as it were, and that is often the case. Whether you make it a rule, some countries like to have lots of rules.
The Aussies always have rules for everything. We tend to be a bit more freewheeling, but that's something that we have considered. New question, Reuben. Thank you. And thank you, minister

Do you still have conversations with your board chairs? Yes. You do. Do you take notes? No. Do you record the boards? No. Do you know if they record the boards? No, I don't know, but I would assume that they don't. Have you issued, since the conversation with the new chair of TVNZ, have you issued any advice or guidance to your chairs in your system around what's in scope and what's out of scope for conversations?

No. Do you think you possibly could have or should have? No. Why would I do that? Given that she had raised an issue that if you had continued the conversation, would have seen you in breach of the broadcasting act. Do you not think for yourself and chair- Well- that space Well, yes, maybe. But look, I think it's pretty fundamental.

To do my role properly, I appoint a number of chairs across a number of organizations, and I see it very much as my job to just call them up randomly at random times and check what's going on. And sometimes to put them on the spot and, "What are you focused on? What's happening here? What's going on?" And I see that very much as my role. And I don't carry a little notebook around with me when I do it.

And it's about the sort of free flow of discussion, and that's the way I sort of operate and seems to work. Do you think do you think that it risks creating a section or cover that a minister could sleep outside of the law and have a conversation around subject matter for news coverage content, and that there would be no evidence that they had or hadn't done?

Yes, but the problem is if you take that sort of line, then it would be safer never to talk to your chair, or to only do it when you've got a formal meeting in place. And that may make you safer, but I've got every confidence that the people involved and myself, we know which is the right side of the line to be on, and we have conversations, and we need to do that. Otherwise, the most common topic of conversation, one of the topics of conversation with board chairs is over upcoming appointments to the board.

And I value their direct views. And you've got to have a discussion, "Well, who do we think we need?" And those are the sorts of questions that quite often I talk through and then asking, "How's it going? How's the business going? What's the priorities?" And in the context of Radio New Zealand and TVNZ, the two things that I'm most interested in are, is the audience growing? Because we're paying for this as a country for Radio New Zealand, and we don't pay TVNZ directly, but we own it. And so we want to make sure that they're

attracting an audience and that they're competing well against the alternatives. And then secondly, I want to know whether they've got a plan to increase the levels of trust that New Zealanders have in them. And so those are a couple of themes that I quite often reverse to. Sure. So just to pick up on the point there in that answer around revenue and the ability for RNZ, TVNZ, and any other news outlet in New Zealand to be able to generate sufficient revenue to be viable or ideally return a profit.

Quite some time ago, more than a year ago, you said you were going to take immediate action in that space. You've since said that you are watching closely the moves in Australia to introduce their equivalent of a fair digital news bargaining bill. How closely are you watching that, and how much longer do you need to watch that for before you're going to be able to do something, anything, that will ensure that there is a revenue stream for news content created in New Zealand that is currently taken and shared on online

platforms with no revenue source for local Although the question is standard, that wasn't a supplementary and a primary. I thank you, Mr Chair. Well, I can advise the member that I continue to watch Australia very closely, and continue to be conscious of the challenges that new technology brings across the sector. We've done what we can. Probably been more helpful, I suppose, in the broadcasting space than the print space.

We've been able to do away with out-of-date laws when it comes to Sunday advertising and there've been other gains made. But in terms of the Fair Digital, so-called, marketing bill that's still on the books but is not being progressed at the moment.

But we're watching how it unfolds in Australia. Minister, I'll ask a couple of questions just sort of on a regional lens, which is of particular interest to those of us who are electorate MPs, and particularly representing large electorate parts of the country. What is the strategy to ensure there's good outreach into communities? And I'll just say, for example, Queenstown Lakes, just speaking personally, is the second most creative place in the country according to information index on a per capita basis, and then you-

Second most creative, did you say? Yes, second only to Wellington, ahead of Auckland, I'm pleased to say. And, you can- That's not in relation to accounting. That's- No, it's on a per capita basis. Oh, okay. As a percentage of people employed in the arts and creative sector. And then say in Gore, we just had the 10-day Tussey Country Music Festival.

Yes. And, the country's pre-eminent country music festival. So, we've got these cool things happening. Absolutely. And they're happening all across the country. How does the ministry sort of work with the very long country to ensure that we support these? Well, of course, that is one of the goals of the changes with Creative New Zealand, to get closer to the regions.

For a very long time it's been very Wellington-based and focused and some regions have done better than others. And you're right to point out that there are some regions in New Zealand that are growing substantially faster than the others, and your area around Queenstown is one and Tauranga is another one.

And so, from time to time you need to sort of adjust these things and make sure that everybody's getting a fair crack. So I'd say that. In terms of the media side of things, of course, one of the things that we funded in the last budget was an extra boost to local democracy reporting and open justice.

And that has helped to some modest degree for some more local journalism to be developed and fed into the system and so that's one useful thing we've been able to do. Sup. Yeah. Thank you, Minister. Just in tune with the regional, I guess, lens.

Just want to ask about connectivity and I know that as Broadcasting Minister you have helped along with some towers and some more connectivity around in the regions. That's tomorrow. And, um- Different bike tomorrow. It's more my communications hat although- Yes... in terms of emergencies and media- Media, yes. You did the AM radio- Yes... towers.

Yes. Yes. Lots of time for that in a different meeting. Yes. Well, no, but the only point I'd make in relation, the primary way that we fund media for emergency purposes is through Radio New Zealand, which has the- Yes... the primary task of doing that.

There is also- The contribution... it's worth noting a contribution made by commercial radio, particularly in some of our rural areas, and we acknowledge that, and they would like us to help them more on that front and that's something that we give some thought to and Minister Jones, of course, is responsible for the Regional Infrastructure Fund, which may have some ability to help in some modest way. So there is a bit of a piece of work that we're looking at there.

Thank you. I'll just remind Minister Leigh that the chair is the arbiter of whether a question's in scope or not. Go to Andy, supplementary. Yeah, just a supplementary off your one to provide a bit of balance to, we've got to look after the regions. But also, you did mention the money that gets spent in Wellington, but there are a large number of national institutions which are based in Wellington.

Well, indeed. And it's- And excellent institutions that they are too. Very good. Very good. I'm glad you say so. But I'm really interested in just making sure that you are going to keep the funding to the level, and obviously they've got to raise money themselves, as we've already had Harper mention. But also things like the ballet, Royal New Zealand Ballet and the Symphony Orchestra. Yeah. That they will have the revenue that they need, but they also need to get out around the country as well and showcase what they do.

Yeah, well, I- So not just based in Wellington. No, well, indeed. But, I think a challenge that all such groups are facing is, ultimately they're competing for attention in a world where people have so much in front of them. The Symphony Orchestra is competing against sitting on the couch scrolling their TikTok, ultimately. And I think there will come a time when people get fed up with the scrolling and get back out and go to the symphony.

But the task of all those organizations is to engage people, and to reconnect And make the case for why it's better to get off the couch and go out and listen to a local musician. And we support as best we can, but ultimately, that's the fundamental task that everybody faces.

And you're confident the level of support at the moment and the level that's predicted to be ongoing, because obviously there are changes proposed here, is adequate for what they need to do? Yes. But it's going to require doing things slightly differently in some cases. There may well be a number of entities that all have their own sort of systems and financial systems and HR systems, and we may be having to come up with more efficient ways of doing things.

And that's just the challenge that everybody faces is we've got to find some efficiencies and make sure as much money as possible that we have available is getting into the hands of those who can best use it. New question, Laura. Oh, thank you. My question's about the game developer rebate scheme for new gaming.

Yep. I know there's about $40 million each year allocated for that. I also noted that to be eligible- That's a-... you have to be part of a studio that has a minimum operating expenditure of $250,000, which is quite high if you were, say, a new game developer trying to get into the industry, and this might be a bit technical.

Well, I just think it's the economic development portfolio of the gaming Okay. It's New Zealand on Air is who it's distributed through? Okay. Yes. We can ask that to someone else. Yeah, you can ask. New question. Karori. Thank you. Thank you, Minister. Minister, you keep saying 2% across the board of savings, but can you confirm that this is not the case for the arts?

It is 2%, 5%, 5% for the next three years. I just need artists to properly understand the numbers. By all means. What you need to understand is it's 2% this year. Well, that's there. And then, the Minister of Finance and the government's indicated that the next two years, we'll be looking for 5% savings across the board.

But the discussions, the debates, the who's it coming from, how it's actually going to operate, is very much what we're going to be focused on in the next few months and if re-elected. So where the 2% has hit and where it's taken place is absolutely right there. The 2%, 5%, yes, we've got to find those savings across government, and including in this portfolio, but exactly how, where, is still something very much to be considered and resolved. And it's possible that the nuances between different areas of government might be different.

So it's not all set in stone. Thank you, Minister, for clarifying that. So there's 5% in year two, 5% in the year three, which is different projections to the majority of the ministries, which say 2% and 2%. And I just want to really be clear, do you think that artists have it easy in this country?

No. And what do you say- Some do, some don't... what do you say to them about the fact that there's going to be even less support? Well, I'd say that New Zealand continues to support the arts very substantially, as we certainly are $1.7 billion over the next four years. So New Zealanders love the arts. They engage with the arts.

And we continue to support as best we can as a country. And to the extent that we're doing well as a country, and we're generating income, the more we can continue to invest in these things. And it's not a nice-to-have, it's an essential. Having a strong cultural framework for the country is very important. That's why we continue to fund it.

Supplementary, Morgan. Minister, just to follow up on that question. Some of the artists in West Coast Tasman who are quite independent and make quite a nice living with their carving and their painting and their jewelry making and other things, are they still able to look for government funding should they so wish?

Well yes, there's- Sorry to make you choke, but There's various funds available, but I think the point you make is there's a wide range, but the creative sector is very broad. There is a substantial group of people who are, in effect, small business people trying to make a living.

They're creating a product, whether it's a painting, piece of jewelry, fashion, a whole range of things, stories, that people are prepared to buy, pay for, and you can make a living doing that. And that's a great way to make a living. It can be challenging, though. And you're competing against all sorts of other things in a pretty tough world. So that's why we have some funds available, but it's never enough to cover everybody's desires.

And at the moment, we're going through a period where we're trying to get ourselves back into a steady state when it comes to our finances. But nevertheless, we continue to invest very substantially in the arts and will continue to do so. New question, Ruby. Thank you, Chair. Radio New Zealand faces 5.636 million in cuts over the next four years.

So a couple of questions on that. At what point does that start hitting newsroom capacity at RNZ? Well, we've got every confidence that they were-- They did have a substantial increase under the previous administration. And we've taken back some of that.

But they've also continued to invest. I think I'm going up on Friday to open up the new facilities in Auckland, which is an empty floor that TVNZ had, which is now being used much more effectively. And that's a very exciting proposition for RNZ. And I think there'll be many opportunities for synergies between those two companies once that's up and running.

So, I think there's a very bright future for Radio New Zealand. And they've made real progress over the last few years in terms of their reach across New Zealand with the success of their app and their news feeds running through various channels.

So, I don't think anybody can say that Radio New Zealand is not having a real impact in terms of its core task in New Zealand. That they face competitive pressures on their morning report flagship. And so I put plenty of pressure on them to ask themselves how they're going to sort of do better, how they're going to grow their audience there.

And with every confidence, they can do that with the budget we've allowed them. So given that pressure that you've spoken about that RNZ experiences, but that TVNZ also experiences. And every other media outlet. I think you'll find NZME and Stuff and all those others all suffer- Absolutely... probably much more substantial economic pressures.

Not Crown-owned companies or not- No... not in the vote, not- Sure... not in your jurisdiction. Sure. But can you assure us that the financial independence on the Crown will never be used intentionally or otherwise as leverage over editorial decisions? Oh, no. Of course not. They're independent and they're a very important part of our democracy because of that.

We certainly would never claim to deliver things from the podium of proof in this government. So- Hear hear. No, last applies. Minister, the reductions are inevitable, whether they be 2%, 5%, anything. With that, the question really is, are these entities, are the sector or the sectors across this spot, arts, culture and heritage, expected to continue to deliver the outputs that they have over these previous four years, over the previous terms, with a reduction of funding?

With the funding, I'll rephrase that, funding being reduced, with funding being reduced, does that then equate to ministers, the minister, and the government reducing its expectations on each of these entities and their outputs? So, or is the minister and the government still expecting the same output with less money?

Well, expecting the same output with less money is one way of describing expecting an increase in productivity. And that may be appropriate in some places, it may be less appropriate in other places. But, yes, we do expect different entities to be more efficient with the resources that they have and deliver excellent outcomes.

And there'll be a range of ways of doing that. But like I say, the 2% savings are fully expected and required. We will be having a debate over the next couple of years of exactly where and how to deal with the future savings that we need to have as a government in order to, as an important part of our path back to surplus.

And so, those discussions are still to be had. And how exactly that falls is something that's very much up for debate over the next few years. Thank you, Minister. We will now ask you to switch hats to the Minister for Pacific Peoples. No, no, switch your officials. There.

See. Cheers for that. Thank you. Thank you. Last involved. He's like, "I'm just going to sit here." Really? You know how it goes. It's not like you, Carmen. With 15 minutes, we don't have much time. But we should be fine. Yeah. Right. Well, thank you, Minister, and welcome back with your Minister for Pacific Peoples hat on. I'm not sure if you're being joined by officials.
All right. It's okay. What? I'm all man. Let's go. I'm sure you can handle it. We got this.

Here we go. All right. Well, shall we just ask you just to do a brief introduction of those who are with you and introduction to the portfolio? No. And from there, we'll move to questions. Well, warm Pacific greetings, talofa lava, malo e lelei, ni sa bula, vinaka, kia orana, fakalofa la tu atu, molo ni, noa ea, and greetings to you all this afternoon. And I'm joined here by the Secretary of Pacific Peoples, Geraldine Clifford-Lindson, and her team, who are all here ready to answer any particular questions

that you have. Obviously, relatively new to this role, but I've had a wonderful couple of months meeting different groups across the sector as I have. And very conscious of the fact that we- One of the stats that have stuck with me is that roughly 25% or a quarter of the kids at school in Auckland today are Pacific Island.

Yep. And a huge pool of talent that there is there. And our future as a country, and certainly as Aucklanders, but it depends on them doing well and achieving their goals and aspirations. And so everything we do as a government is designed to ensure that. If you think of education policy around getting kids into school, or if you think of our health policy around ensuring that young kids are vaccinated and given every encouragement to succeed, and right across the board. So it's a core element of
everything we do in government. And then we have the ministry, which is a modest size ministry with a clear

goal of being a center of excellence within the government apparatus, with a set of connections and understanding of the Pacific peoples in New Zealand. And also running a series of programs that have been funded in the past, some of which will continue, some of which will be folded into more general government programs as time progresses. And happy to answer any questions.

Thank you, Minister. First questions, Carmel. Great. We were made aware of the fact that you, as the Minister for Pacific Peoples, have a performance plan that is largely to do with cost savings and how you're going to run the agency with respect to some of the savings that the government are seeking to make. But that's not currently publicly available. Can you speak to that a little bit, Minister?

Well, as I was talking about in the previous session, in terms of the 2% savings that are expected right across the board, and I'll remind people who are tuning in for this particular session, across the country and across the Pacific maybe, on the shortwave tuning in, that what we're doing here is the annual budget scrutiny. And the hard, cold fact is that notwithstanding all the efforts that have been made so far, New Zealand is still going to be borrowing over $11 billion in this budget. We have a deficit of over $11 billion, and we've got a

path back to surplus over the next three years. And no country can continue to spend more than it earns for a long period of time without just passing the bill to the next generation. We don't think that's right. We don't think that's right, and that's why we've got a plan to get back. So, part of that is an expectation on all government departments to have a 2% savings in place. And the Pacific peoples are no different to that.

And do you think that it's right that that's not publicly available and that there's no transparency around your performance plan as the minister, particularly given the officials are expected to respond to that and there are indicators attached to it?

Well, I'm not quite sure where the mystery is. I thought it's well known that we're looking for 2% savings. No, just the plan. So is there- How they're going to get there is, well, that's something that we're all working out together. And I've got every confidence that the secretary will be able to achieve that.

The plans aren't due until later this year, so we're still under active consideration. But you're working to a performance plan that the minister has set for cost savings? We're in the planning process and just thinking about, remember the parameters are to 2029. So we're just working through what the pathway would be, and that will be due later this year.

Are there already indicators for that, though? No. We're just working through that process. Okay. Second question very quickly. I'll come back to you. Okay. Yeah. Well, I've got a sup on that one, but can I sup- I'll sup and then go for a question... maybe ask a quick question? Yeah. When are those due? What? Uh- Just this plan. So what we're asking for is transparency on the plan.

We want to see it. We understand you say it's in draft. When is it due so we can have a look? It's a sup, and then I've got a primary. Have you got the date on you? 29th of July is when it's due to Treasury, and then it will go to Cabinet for consideration. So it hasn't been considered by Cabinet- So 29th of July I'll see it then. Well, that's when we get it. That's when we have to submit it.

Not when you get it. It might take a little bit longer. Might take a little bit longer. Okay, my primary is around the announcement of 9,000 jobs being cut. So I want to know- Yeah... thinking around whose job's going to get cut at the Ministry of Pacific Peoples, and how is that going to impact the program? Well, I think the member is referring to the overall public sector job changes. And again, people who are tuning in, back in 2017 when John Key and Bill English and the previous National government left office, there

were 47,000 public servants, core public servants. When we came back six years later, there were 63,000. So, there'd been a very substantial increase in the number of public servants. And people might ask, "Well, were we getting better outcomes from that?" Um- In terms of the core activities of the state.

And so, that was a significant increase over the long term, so the historic average. And so what we set as a goal was to get back to 55,000. So if we look in terms of Pacific peoples, I just looked at the-- I asked about the head count, so back in 2017 there were 37,000 people working for Pacific...

Uh, 37 people. 37 people, full stop. And by 2023, when we came back, there was 138 people. So, we had tripled. And so, there may be some readjustment. We're back to 84 now. And so I don't know the exact sort of makeup of the numbers. But the reality was, and the members here need to take acknowledgement of that, there was a hiring spree over an extended period of time. And that was one of the reasons why we're running massive deficits as a country, and why we as a government have to do something about it.

So my question was specifically to do with the announcements from the Ministry of Finance, who specifically talked about the 9,000 jobs within this time period. Yes. So how is that going to impact the Ministry for Pacific Peoples? Well, it will impact the Ministry of Pacific Peoples- But exactly what number has not been allocated... you haven't got the numbers yet. No. When will you have the numbers?

Well, she just- Well, that's something that will be... We've got a period of time to get to that figure. And I expect the ministry, like every other ministry, to be- Okay... working away to see what's the best way to achieve that. And there's lots of different elements to that.

It may well be that there are some operations that can be shared with other ministries. There may be looking back to how do we manage to do things in 2017 with a certain number of people, and how can we get back to that. I have a new line of questioning.

I'm interested whether, and this may be an operational question, but I'm interested whether there's any thinking yet about AI, because this is obviously reshaping our world at scale and pace right now. And obviously with the Pacific community, we've got the biggest single city on the planet in Auckland. And so is there any thinking about what role, if any, the ministry would play in an AI strategy, both for that community and also the broader diaspora across the Pacific?

Well, go to the secretary, but there's no question that we're living in a revolution of sorts as we speak in terms of technology. One of the most amazing areas is at a very simple, well, not a simple level, but has been the ability to translate almost simultaneously across many, many languages.

And I can only imagine that continuing to grow. And so that raises enormous opportunities. And also challenges of course, because as larger, wealthier countries get better access to computing power, we've got to find a way to make sure that we in New Zealand get access to as much as we can.

And also, the broader Pacific policy. But that's more in the scope of foreign affairs. But in terms of our Pasifika community here in New Zealand, that's very much core function of our broader education system as well as everything else. But is there anything you particularly wanted to add on that?

Yeah. So firstly, our workforce in the ministry reflects the Pacific population. It's a young workforce. It's a workforce that fits the future. There's huge opportunity for them to contribute to the public service. So our focus hasn't really been on downsizing, it's been about capability build and building resilience around change. Part of that is our AI program.

81% of our staff are already actively using AI. Last week alone, we had 3,000 weekly prompts and... So staff are highly active in the future. And this is really important for us to build that confidence so that they are highly competitive. That's great to hear. I'll just ask one supplementary on that.

Because there's sort of been fears that AI will lead to job losses, but I think what companies are realizing overseas is that the additional productivity that's coming from the use of that tool actually requires more people often. So just, is there sort of any thinking yet about not just the use internally of the tool, but also increasing awareness in the Pasifika community about the value of AI as a productivity enabler?

Well, look, I'm sure there are many opportunities. Yes, different ways of seeing it. But in terms of as a government, there are many, many things that we struggle to do and have struggled to do for a very long period of time. One of them is measuring the success or otherwise of various government programs that we have.

And the use of technology and data to more effectively understand that is one of the huge opportunities that I think AI will bring right across the public sector. And Pacific peoples is no different to anybody else in that regard. So, huge opportunities there. And so, I can get off topic on that one there but yeah.

All right. New question, ordinately. Minister, the numbers you espoused before about Pasifika people, especially here in Aotearoa, they're nearing the half a million mark, 442,000 at the last census count in 2023. That was also the year you say there was a huge boost or pop- In terms of personnel at the ministry, and now it's been reduced by about 57 in 2026, so the numbers have gone down. There was a huge spike, I understand, but still, there's huge- Still more than double what it was in 2017.

What I'm getting to, the point is, is that we have not only Pasifika, but Māori disproportionately represented across every system within government control, within each facet specifically. For the Ministry for Pacific Peoples, with the decrease and the reductions, especially within this ministry, what assurances can you give to all New Zealanders, not just Pacific peoples, but Māori and all peoples across Aotearoa, that you have Pasifika peoples' backs as the minister, and that the ministry is actually safe under your leadership?

Well, the assurance I'll give people is that this is a government that is focused on fixing the basics and building a future where all New Zealanders, including Pasifika, are well-equipped to succeed. And so, one of the most basic things you can do in the health setting is ensure that kids are properly immunized. And notwithstanding all the extra people employed by the previous administration, immunization rates went down substantially.

We used to have, I don't have the exact figure in front of me, about 94% of Pasifika kids immunized when we left office in 2017, and it just fell off a cliff, notwithstanding all the extra money that the previous government was throwing around. And so we're focused on getting that back up, and we've made real progress since. Same thing with attendance at school, which is the most fundamental thing to foot, to actually make some progress in education and be equipped to succeed in the modern world.

And we had lamentable attendance rates over the last six years, and we are focused on turning that around. Okay. I can give that assurance to anybody tuning in, that this is a government that is focused on fixing the basics, and equipping Pasifika people to succeed. Supplementary: How do you then intend to achieve that when this specific ministry has suffered 16 million, six zero, $16 million worth of cuts over the last three years, and now they're also undergoing further cuts?

Because I think we fundamentally don't agree with the prescription that the previous administration had, which was that you had to have a little separately funded program in place under the label of the Pacific Peoples Ministry, and that would somehow automatically lead to better outcomes.

A lot of what we do is just core business of the government, such as employment schemes and housing schemes, and so there was money going in separately into the ministry for those projects, and we think they can be done better as a core activity of the government. But still, notwithstanding that, having the expertise and understanding that the Ministry of Pacific Peoples provides.

New question, Andy. Well, mine's- Okay... it's a new question, but it's also in the same vein. Just really interested in what success looks like for you, and your response there has both been about health and education. But what success looks like and also the role of the Ministry of Pacific Peoples in delivering that success as opposed to the role of any other agencies of government. Obviously, it's the interface, I suppose, between the role of the Ministry of Pacific Peoples and, in this case, you've talked about health and

education. There could be other ministries as well. Yeah. Well, success looks like Pasifika New Zealanders, those, like I say, the quarter of kids in Auckland schools being equipped to succeed in a modern, successful, rich economy in which we live in. And we're a party that in a center-right sort of philosophy of equality of opportunities, and equipping young people to succeed, and then having good quality public services that everybody can address

and access. And so, there is a bit of a different sort of philosophy across governments. There's a tendency under Labour-led governments to sort of separate- You don't need to talk about us You just talk about your own program and- It's not been critical, necessarily. It's just that- Will you go back to the estimates chair? Hey, hey, hey, hey.

There's a tendency to go that way. We're going the other way, which is to say- On the answer of this, Carmel... we see an opportunity to have the ministry as, like I say, a center of excellence for across government understanding of Pasifika, with connections into the community and also- Sel... from where appropriate, particular projects that need to be developed.

So you see the Ministry of Pacific Peoples being essentially an advisor to the delivery agencies- Well, I wouldn't quite go that far. But that's an important part of the role, yes. Supplementary. One, it'll have to be last question, supplementary. Yeah. Well, it was going to be a primary question, but off the back of the question that the member Evan Foster has asked, what advice have you been giving the Minister for Employment on Pacific unemployment, given that employment for Pacific peoples is at 11.9%? So what advice has the Ministry for Pacific Peoples been giving that

particular ministry on Pacific unemployment? Well, there's ongoing discussions around the transfer of particular projects to Aotearoa. And- I'm not asking about projects, policy advice Yeah. Well, you can ask the secretary around specific policy advice, but the advice I'd be giving to my cabinet colleagues is carry on doing what you're doing in the sense of making-- The best thing we can do long-term for Pasifika unemployment is to ensure that more Pasifika kids are at school, and coming out of their school

fully qualified and prepared to succeed in the modern world. And then the second most useful thing we can do is ensure that we've got a growing economy that is creating jobs. And the good news of the budget was the prediction that New Zealand will be creating, I think, around 200,000 jobs over the next three years.

And all of those 200,000 jobs present an opportunity for young Pasifika kids to get off the dole and into work. And we'd back them every step of the way. Thank you, Minister. Thank you for your time, and thank you for official's time this afternoon. Appreciate it. Thank you. Thank you.
